era_context: Late 14th century: Ottoman Empire consolidating Balkan power under Murad I; Ming Dynasty under Zhu Yuanzhang in mature phase; Goryeo Dynasty in decline; Timur's empire at its height in Central Asia; Western Schism ongoing; English political stability under Richard II.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (no contemporary records)
Southeast Asia (limited European documentation; local records sparse)

events.title: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans under Murad I
events.description: Ottoman Sultan Murad I continued his military campaigns in the Balkans, expanding Ottoman control over Serbian and Bulgarian territories. In 1386, he was consolidating administrative control over conquered lands and preparing for further expansion. His reign (1362–1389) represented the transformation of the Ottoman state from a regional power to a major imperial force that would dominate the eastern Mediterranean for centuries.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion during Murad I's reign is well-documented, but specific events in 1386 are not individually detailed in surviving sources. This entry reflects the documented pattern of Ottoman military activity during this period.

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidation under Hongwu Emperor
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor) was consolidating Ming power through administrative reforms and the elimination of potential rivals. In 1386, he was implementing the examination system, reorganizing provincial administration, and strengthening central authority. His reign (1368–1398) established the bureaucratic and institutional foundations of the Ming Dynasty that would persist for nearly three centuries.
events.certainty_note: Zhu Yuanzhang's administrative consolidation is well-documented in Ming official records. The year 1386 falls within his documented period of systematic bureaucratic reorganization.

events.title: Goryeo Dynasty in political fragmentation
events.description: The Goryeo Dynasty was experiencing severe political fragmentation, with competing factions vying for power. In 1386, the court was weakened by internal conflicts and the rising influence of military generals. The year 1386 represents the period immediately preceding Yi Seong-gye's rise to prominence and eventual coup in 1388. Goryeo's decline was marked by loss of territorial control and administrative coherence.

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Kings)

events.certainty_note: Goryeo's political fragmentation in the late 14th century is well-documented in Korean historical records. The year 1386 falls clearly within the documented period of Goryeo's decline.

events.title: Timur consolidates Central Asian empire
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) was consolidating his vast empire and establishing Samarkand as his capital and cultural center. In 1386, he was commissioning architectural and artistic projects that would make Samarkand one of the great cultural centers of the Islamic world. His reign represented a synthesis of Mongol military traditions and Persian-Islamic cultural patronage.
key_figures: Ulugh Beg (Timur's grandson, future astronomer and ruler)

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafar-nama (Book of Victories)

events.certainty_note: Timur's general activities and cultural patronage are well-documented, but specific events in 1386 are not precisely dated. Archaeological evidence from Samarkand confirms his building projects during this period.

events.title: Richard II consolidates English royal authority
events.description: King Richard II (r. 1377–1399) was consolidating his authority in England following the Peasants' Revolt (1381). In 1386, he was strengthening royal power and managing relationships with the nobility. The year 1386 represents a period of relative stability before the political crisis of 1388–1389 when the Lords Appellant would challenge his authority.
key_figures: John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, Richard's uncle)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Richard II's reign and the political situation in 1386 are well-documented in English royal records and contemporary chronicles.

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Ottoman 'expansion' in 1386 may overstate the coherence and inevitability of Ottoman military success. Recent scholarship emphasizes the role of Balkan Christian states' internal fragmentation and the gradual nature of Ottoman consolidation rather than rapid conquest. The Hongwu Emperor's administrative reforms are often portrayed as purely centralizing, but recent scholarship emphasizes the persistence of regional power and the limits of centralization even under Zhu Yuanzhang's strong rule. The characterization of Timur's cultural patronage as a 'synthesis' of Mongol and Persian traditions may obscure the complex and contested nature of cultural identity in the Timurid empire.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1386 is moderate but regionally uneven. East Asian sources (Ming Shilu, Goryeo-sa) are contemporary or near-contemporary. Ottoman and Central Asian sources are primarily later compilations. English sources are contemporary and detailed. There is a Western bias in available documentation, with Ottoman, Central Asian, and East Asian sources receiving less attention in Western historiography. The year 1386 is less well-documented than 1389 (Battle of Kosovo) or 1392 (Joseon founding), suggesting that historical attention clusters around dramatic events. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania have no documented written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Ottoman expansion in 1386 continued into 1387 and set conditions for the Battle of Kosovo Polje in 1389
Hongwu Emperor's consolidation in 1386 continued into 1387 and beyond
Goryeo's fragmentation in 1386 deepened in 1387 and led to Yi Seong-gye's coup in 1388
Richard II's consolidation in 1386 was followed by political tensions in 1387
